dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 87 for abdominal pain at 1420 Main Street, Apt C.
Hello sir, I'm EMT Ryan. My partner is Anna. What seems to be the problem today?
He is experiencing severe abdominal pain and needs immediate assistance.
Mr. Ramirez, can you answer a few questions for us?
His eyes are open and he's looking around actively, despite the pain.
Mr. Ramirez, please tell me your name and age if you can.
Juan is 62 years old and is moving his arms and legs, though he grimaces with pain.
Airway is clear, no obstruction, and Mr. Ramirez is speaking intermittently through the pain.
Mr. Ramirez, are you having difficulty breathing or chest pain?
He's not having any difficulty breathing and says there's no chest pain right now.
He's alert and oriented. Skin is pink, warm, and dry, no signs of cyanosis or pallor.
His pulse is 102 and regular. Normal for now, but faster than average.
His respirations are 20 per minute and unlabored. Looks comfortable breathing.
Blood pressure reading is 151 over 93. Slightly elevated but stable so far.
Blood glucose is very high-339 mg/dL. Important to note in a diabetic patient.
SpO2 is at 95% on room air. No signs of hypoxia, but keep this under watch during transport.
Bystander, can you tell me when Juan first started feeling pain?
According to him, it started about two hours ago after he drank some beers.
How would he describe the pain, and where is it located?
It's a sharp pain in the middle of his stomach, near the belly button.
Does he have a history of any chronic conditions we should know about?
Yes, he has chronic pancreatitis, an old myocardial infarction, gout, and type 1 diabetes.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how severe is the pain right now?
He says it's a 10-it's the worst he's felt so far.
Mr. Ramirez, does anything make the pain better or worse?
It gets worse with touch-he doesn't like palpation attempts at all.
Medic Partner, check for rigidity and guarding during palpation.
Guarding is noted. He tenses his abdomen involuntarily when touched.
Bystander, does the pain go anywhere else, like the back or shoulder?
No radiation reported. It stays focused in the center of his stomach.
12-lead EKG performed. Results show sinus tachycardia, no ST elevation.
Partner, let's establish an IV in the left AC if possible.
20G IV secured in the left antecubital. Flush and flow is good.
Starting a 300 mL normal saline intravenous bolus. Protocol allows this.
IV will remain open and monitored throughout the transport.
Activate and label the continuous monitoring device, please.
Family, we will now carefully lift Mr. Ramirez to the stretcher.
They are helping to move him. Be careful with his abdomen.
Stretcher locked and ready for transport. Call sign standing by.
Follow-up vitals-pulse at 89, much lower and more regular.
Blood pressure now is 131 over 82. More stable and under control.
SpO2 is now up to 100%. Continued saturation is encouraging.
Final EKG is clear-showing baseline sinus rhythm with no elevation or ectopy.
Guarding is still present, but no signs of tenderness outside of the abdomen.
Following protocol for medical-abdominal pain. No field interventions beyond IV and saline.
Calling for transport to Chippenham ED for further care.
Stretcher is ready. Transport team releasing for emergency department.
Transport decision made to Chippenham ED, Bed 15. En route.
Mr. Ramirez is now under the care of the nurse in Bed 15. Here's the report.
Thank you, we are reviewing the report now and have him secure and monitored.
